root.narration: Runner on second, nobody out, your team leads by one. You're the pitcher. You deliver a pitch and the batter lines a single to right field! The right fielder fields it cleanly and the runner on second is rounding third, heading home! The right fielder is throwing to home plate. You just pitched the ball -- your pitching job is done for this play. But what's your FIELDING job now?

choices.text: Sprint behind home plate! If the throw from right field gets past the catcher, I need to be there to stop it!
Stay on the mound. My job is to pitch, not run around.
Run to cover third base in case the batter tries to advance.

outcome-backup-great.narration: You sprint to the area behind home plate, about 20 feet behind the catcher, right in line with the throw coming from right field. The throw from right field is a little high -- it bounces off the catcher's glove! But YOU'RE RIGHT THERE! You scoop up the ball and toss it to the catcher. The runner scores, but the batter stays at first because you prevented the ball from rolling to the backstop. Without your backup, the batter would have taken second or third!

outcome.headline: Great Backup! Prevented Extra Bases!
outcome.explanation: After you deliver a pitch and the ball is hit to the outfield, your job as a pitcher CHANGES. You're no longer a pitcher -- you're the backup fielder. On any throw to home plate from the outfield, you sprint behind home plate and position yourself about 15-20 feet behind the catcher, in a direct line with the throw. If the throw gets past the catcher (which happens a LOT), you're there to stop it from rolling to the backstop. Without a backup, an overthrow at home can turn a single into a triple. This is pure hustle and awareness -- and great pitchers do it on EVERY play.

keyTerms.definition: When the pitcher sprints behind the catcher to stop overthrows from the outfield. Position yourself 15-20 feet behind the catcher, in line with the throw.
After you deliver the pitch, you become the fifth infielder. You have defensive responsibilities on every batted ball -- covering bases, fielding bunts, and backing up throws.
Standing directly behind the target on a straight line from where the throw is coming. This way the ball comes right to you if it gets through.

outcome.whatToRemember: After you pitch, your job isn't done! On outfield hits with a play at home, sprint behind the catcher to back up the throw. This hustle prevents extra bases and shows your team you're a complete player.

outcome-stay-bad.narration: You stand on the mound and watch the play. The right fielder throws home -- it's a little offline and bounces off the catcher's glove! The ball rolls all the way to the backstop! Nobody is behind the catcher! The runner scores, and now the batter sees the loose ball and sprints to second, then to THIRD! What was a single with a runner scoring is now a runner scoring AND the batter on third -- all because nobody backed up home plate!

outcome.headline: No Backup! Ball to Backstop! Extra Bases!
outcome.explanation: Standing on the mound watching the play is one of the biggest mistakes a pitcher can make. The moment the ball is hit, you become a FIELDER. You have responsibilities just like every other player on the field. On throws to home plate, YOU are the designated backup. Nobody else can get there in time. The catcher is at the plate, the first baseman is on their bag, the third baseman is on theirs. The only player who can sprint behind home plate in time is the pitcher. Coaches notice pitchers who hustle to back up plays, and they REALLY notice pitchers who stand on the mound doing nothing.
outcome.whatToRemember: NEVER stand on the mound watching! The moment the ball is hit, you're a fielder. Sprint to back up the base where the throw is going. Hustle is never optional.

outcome-third-okay.narration: You sprint to cover third base. That's not wrong -- it's a legitimate fielding play. But the throw from right field goes to HOME, not third. The throw gets past the catcher and nobody is backing up home plate. The ball rolls to the backstop and the batter advances to second. The runner scores, which was probably going to happen anyway, but the batter gained an extra base because home plate wasn't backed up.

outcome.headline: Wrong Base to Back Up! Home Plate Needed You!
outcome.explanation: Covering third base shows good instincts -- you're thinking like a fielder. But on this play, the throw was going HOME, not to third. The rule of thumb is: back up the base where the THROW is going. If the outfielder is throwing home, you back up home. If the outfielder is throwing to third, you back up third. Before you start running, read the play: where is the throw going? That's where you need to be behind. The third baseman can cover their own bag -- but nobody else can back up home plate except you.
outcome.whatToRemember: Back up the base where the THROW is going. On throws to home, back up the catcher. On throws to third, back up third base. Read the play first, then run!
